chapters.title: A long-standing divide separates the church from the LGBTQ community
chapters.text:
LGBTQ is a wide label that covers people who are not heterosexual or not cisgender. The group includes lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, and queer individuals who question their sexuality. Across history these people have faced harsh persecution for how they live. Much of that persecution came from the church. Being gay or queer was treated as a crime against God and against the government. Father Martin uses his wide knowledge to find a way to bring this pushed-aside group back into the fold and keep them free of any condemnation. The church and its foundation frown on same-sex relationships, so the church has long been the last place an LGBTQ member wants to be. The church has stood at the front of the condemnation and has helped stir up the fires of persecution. Father Martin wants to close this divide or lay a bridge across it. Using the catechism and the life Jesus lived, he builds a template that readers can follow on how to relate better with LGBTQ people. Christ’s teachings show us how to treat the LGBTQ with greater care.

In today’s world there is more inclusion and many people are more open, yet LGBTQ members are still pressed down and persecuted. Much like bigotry, the church and its members have judged them as flawed and broken. Some feel they do not deserve God’s love. That notion will be broken apart as we read on.

chapters.title: Respect for every person and the choices they make is essential
chapters.text:
One key way to shift our mindset toward the LGBTQ community is to remember that every human is made in God’s image. This means that every person deserves our respect whether gay or straight. The life of a gay or lesbian individual does not affect the life of a straight one. The continuing hatred and persecution grows out of disrespect for their basic humanity. When we show others the respect they deserve, it is also a gesture toward God. At our core, whether straight or gay, we are human, and that is what we all share. To clear our minds of this unfounded hate and disdain for the LGBTQ, we must be ready and willing to show respect, first for their humanity and then for their right to live life as it pleases them. The choices of the LGBTQ bring us no harm, so they do not deserve the negativity and persecution that come their way. When we go out of our way to show respect for them and for their choices, we find there is little space left for hatred or judgment. When we respect, we extend a kind of pass that keeps us from passing judgment in the usual way. We must be open to showing respect and accepting the LGBTQ as normal people.

The church and society at large must be willing to respect LGBTQ members and welcome them into the fold. The church has a moral duty to be a safe place for sinners and those who are thought to have gone astray. If there is real concern behind the judgment, the church is the perfect place for any change that is needed.

chapters.title: If we belong to God, we must show the compassion Christ taught
chapters.text:
More than we know, our actions and our silences around the LGBTQ are dangerous. Whether we speak up to condemn or stay quiet when they face persecution, we are equally complicit. They do not deserve to be hunted down and hurt just because of how they were born. They deserve a little compassion from us. We cannot ignore the plight of the LGBTQ and call ourselves Christ-like. Compassion is sympathetic pity and concern for the suffering or misfortune of others. It is the reaction in the face of an unfortunate plight that prompts us to offer help or some relief from their situation. It was Christ’s nature when he walked the earth, and it should be the way we live each day. These persecuted people deserve compassion, and we must make sure we show it. Compassion is tied to our Lord Jesus. He healed many people out of the compassion he felt. He was moved by pity and empathy and only sought to put an end to their suffering. We too must embody him in our daily lives, showing only compassion when we relate to or interact with LGBTQ members. Jesus laid out the examples for how we must treat our neighbors, and we must follow them to the letter. When we show compassion, we no longer see these people as a scourge or plague. Instead we see them as lost and in need of guidance. We must then make sure this guidance is offered in love and not to condemn. Christ would not judge. He would save.

A 2013 survey found that 92 percent of adults who identified as LGBTQ said they believed society had become more accepting of them than in the past ten years.

chapters.title: Sensitivity has an important place in relating to the LGBTQ community
chapters.text:
Even as we make intentional moves to open the church doors to LGBTQ members, we cannot rush without first raising awareness in the fold. Everyone must be very familiar with the LGBTQ, what the term means, and who belongs to it. This way they will be more sensitive to their struggles and needs. Without real knowledge about the LGBTQ, it is hard to expect or ask for sensitivity. Without a sensitized and sensitive mindset, the church and society will simply slip back into old patterns of judgment and persecution once they let the LGBTQ in. To avoid this, they must be made familiar with the topic. We mostly hate what we do not understand, so to erase hate we must welcome truth. When there is a lack of knowledge, acceptance into the fold would be shallow at best. The truth is that we do not know enough about the LGBTQ to judge or criticize them, and we must hold back from making uneducated statements or holding ill-informed opinions. A mind that is willing to learn and unlearn is truly intelligent. We must welcome growth and learn to be more sensitive to the plight of the LGBTQ. If you get the chance to learn about the LGBTQ, you will gain more appreciation for them. Becoming sensitive does not mean you have to convert or lose your identity. It simply means you can express empathy, which is a valid human emotion, toward them. As humans, we must all be more compassionate and sensitive, especially toward the LGBTQ.

Father James Martin once noted that it is impossible to experience a person’s life, or to be compassionate, if you do not listen to the person or if you do not ask questions.

chapters.title: Opening the doors to the LGBTQ is also an act of compassion toward the church
chapters.text: When we accept that LGBTQ people are humans just like us and deserve our respect, compassion, and acceptance, it is important that we too are willing, open, and ready to receive the same treatment from them. It is not far-fetched to expect compassion or empathy from the LGBTQ toward the church, regardless of their shared history. The place of compassion in the reconciliation of the LGBTQ with the church cannot be overstated. The church has been deeply committed to persecuting and alienating its LGBTQ members since time began, and that has made the church the number one enemy. For generations there has been a great disconnect, a gap, between the LGBTQ and the church. The gap is not because the former hates the latter but because the latter has tried to wipe out the former. The continuing persecution from the church has pushed the LGBTQ away from it over the years. LGBTQ people are very capable of compassion, respect, and sensitivity if given a chance. Father Martin’s main point is that LGBTQ people are humans first and foremost and can only be expected to act and think the same as any straight person, just like you and me. If the church wishes to feel compassion from the LGBTQ, it must be ready to accept them as well. The church must now return the acceptance it would expect from the LGBTQ and embrace their show of compassion, respect, and sensitivity. All they need is a chance to exist and function as part of society without being judged and persecuted. When they are not weighed down by the stigma around them and their life choices, they will be able to be compassionate to the church. Until then, nobody wants to show his oppressor compassion.

chapters.title: Building a bridge between the church and the LGBTQ is essential
chapters.text: The aim of this timely piece of literature is to bridge the gap between the church and LGBTQ members. Father Martin hopes to reach the minds and hearts of the faithful in order to inspire a change in behavior. Building a bridge is a lot of work, but it must be done. The responsibility for reconciliation rests squarely with the church, and it must act accordingly. The main issue is that the church, due to years of indoctrination and a refusal to adapt with the times, has been stuck picking on couples of the same sex who appear to be in a relationship. They have bullied and oppressed them, going as far back as the time when women who did science were called witches. The church must take real steps toward making up for the senseless violence inflicted over the years. Surely this hostility still exists today as the faith has grown into a much larger group than before. The world around it has also evolved, and that has left little room to hide the vile mistreatment of the LGBTQ by the church. Today, though, Father Martin calls for a truce, urging the church to take steps toward reconciliation and making peace with the LGBTQ community. The hatred and persecution continue to this day, but there is hope, and there is room for reconciliation. For a bridge to truly be built to unite both sides, there must be an understanding and a promise on both ends to act in the interest of the other. Without respect, compassion, and sensitivity, the bridge will not hold up, and the gap will widen until there is no way back.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
As humans, it is almost second nature to judge, criticize, and punish, but with the Christian life there is supposed to be a mental revival, a renewing of the mind. The Christian is meant to follow the life of Christ. When Christ walked the earth, he lived an exemplary life and made it a habit to show compassion and pity to those people had counted off. If a person chooses to follow the teachings of Christ, then they must be ready to do as he did. The famous question, What Would Jesus Do, still rings true. Father Martin urges readers to live like Christ and do as he would. In the treatment of the LGBTQ community, the church has done a lot of wrongs, and it will take a long time to reconcile. But there is hope for at least some kind of truce. If there is a place for respect, compassion, and sensitivity, there will be a change in how we treat each other and a more suitable environment for the LGBTQ to live side by side.

In our relations with each other, we must show respect, first for their humanity and their right to choose what to do and who to be. We must also make the effort to be compassionate, showing empathy for them and for how they are misunderstood, and gain sensitivity about their lives, struggles, and choices.

Try this: treat other people, whether straight or gay, with the highest respect, compassion, and sensitivity. No one is less than the other, especially not in God’s eye.
